headline: New Zinc Oxide Design Makes Mineral Sunscreen Invisible on All Skin Tones
meta_description: UCLA researchers redesigned zinc oxide particles to create mineral sunscreen that protects skin without the chalky white cast that discourages daily use.
summary_short: Scientists engineered tetrapod-shaped zinc oxide particles that deliver SPF 30 protection while looking nearly invisible—a breakthrough for skin cancer prevention.
body:
A frustration became innovation. When AJ Addae, a UCLA chemical biology doctoral candidate, found himself avoiding mineral sunscreen because of its chalky white appearance on his skin, he decided to solve the problem that affects millions.

He and his team at UCLA Health's Jonsson Comprehensive Cancer Center discovered that the issue wasn't the zinc oxide itself—a compound the FDA classifies as safe and effective. The problem was its shape. Standard sunscreen uses tiny, roughly spherical nanoparticles that clump together, scattering light and creating that telltale white or gray film.

The researchers engineered zinc oxide into microscopic four-armed structures called tetrapods. Because of their shape, these particles form porous networks instead of collapsing into clumps, staying evenly distributed throughout the sunscreen.

The results, published in ACS Materials Letters, proved remarkable. Tetrapod formulas delivered the same SPF 30 protection as conventional mineral sunscreen while remaining more stable over time. Most importantly, they produced a warmer appearance that matched natural skin tones without added pigments or coatings.

The breakthrough matters beyond cosmetics. People with darker skin tones are significantly more likely to receive melanoma diagnoses at later stages, when treatment becomes harder. If sunscreen looks better, more people will use it consistently—potentially saving lives.

"This isn't just about cosmetics," said senior author Paul S. Weiss, a UCLA distinguished professor. Better appearance could drive better protection.
